item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Quite an original idea. My only nitpick is the researcher's log, which feel a bit too much like a stream of consciousness or writer's notes than what a researcher would actually write in their notes, to the point I wondered if the radio was not impacting his sanity.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For the last time: They are as intended.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Am I imagining things or are the captions below the top two images vice-versa'd from the location that they are supposed to be in?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed that the logs are the weakest part. I think you're on the right track - perhaps a more impersonal log of the object's transfers and frustrated tests by a small group of researchers (passed from General to Sigils to Memetics to Antimemetics) could convey the messy feeling of academia without the personal musings (as I feel like they don't add much to the final punch)?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The images are really brilliant and absolutely make this article. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It took me a second to actually get what the photos were saying, but when I did I actually gasped. That's really, really clever - it stays the same, but changes everything else except we are unaware we've changed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
“In two to three workdays time I'll know, and if it isn't a fluke, I could be looking forward to a 001 Proposal of my own.“

This line is a bit too 4th-wall breaking for my tastes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Man, I'd have to agree on that. The research log was tacked on as a measure to forcefully add a narrative to the article (otherwise it would've been labeled as a 'thing that does thing'. That's what happened to the previous version of this article).

On that thought, I'd like to hear your suggestions on how to improve it. It does have some symbolic merit, as it chronicles some of my ideas on how people would/should react upon first reading SCP-4832, as well as mirroring my own struggles with academia and the hubris amidst which I published this article's first version.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked its initial incarnation, but I love it now. Easy +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is based around an earlier idea of mine in which a cartoon Foundation contained a normal human. The idea felt extremely flat though.

cartoon Foundation

idea felt extremely flat

was that intentional

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The logs in the latter half are rather flat/dry

Agreed. They're by far the worst part of the article. The images make up for them, but only just.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The images are an incredibly nice touch, I am such a sucker for creative and fun images on this website!

1+

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The logs in the latter half are rather flat/dry, but this is a novel enough article that doesn't try to do too much with a simple concept. The image gallery is a really nice touch. This is a good example of not needing to move mountains to tell a story.

It's worth a +1 from me.

The effect here is quite clever and the images really bring the concept home nicely.

Easy +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My second mainlist article, second time posting it. I've made some rewriting and topical additions, the previous lack of which led to unnecessary confusion.

I'd like to thank someauthordude, Tanhony and keon for giving me feedback.

This is based around an earlier idea of mine in which a cartoon Foundation contained a normal human. The idea felt extremely flat though. I instead wanted to have a single object display multiple effects: some small inanimate object, maybe with a dial so that It'll be easy to accommodate any number of possible effects I could think of.

All images in this article are taken by me, feature myself, and have been edited by myself using software which I legally own (Sony Vegas Pro and Sketchbook Pro, respectively). As such, I'm releasing them under the Creative Commons 3.0 licence.

The extent of the actual effects of SCP-4832 is intentionally left ambiguous. My original headcanon with the first iteration of this article was that SCP-4832 changes the appearance of the entire universe. This led to some strange implications though, and my current headcanon is that SCP-4832 only changes its immediate surroundings.
